notes.note: S. D. is salutem dicit, "sends greetings"; CONLEGAE addresses Cornificius as Cicero's fellow-augur, a member of the same priestly college.

Q. Caecilius Bassus, a Pompeian who in 46 BC raised a revolt in Syria and seized the province from Caesar's governor Sex. Iulius Caesar; his next move was the uncertainty Cornificius was waiting to resolve.

notes.anchor: at our friend Caesar's games I watched T. Plancus
notes.note: At Caesar's victory games of 46 BC; T. Plancus is most plausibly T. Munatius Plancus Bursa, the disgraced ex-tribune whom Cicero had once prosecuted, now restored under Caesar, so that watching him is itself part of the new order Cicero finds galling.

notes.anchor: listened to the verses of Laberius and Publilius
notes.note: Decimus Laberius and Publilius Syrus were the leading mime-writers of the day; at these games Caesar forced the elderly knight Laberius to act in his own mimes against Publilius, a calculated humiliation that Cicero watched "with the most equable mind".
